senses.definition: Soothing, calming, or subduing someone or something (present participle of the obsolete verb 'accoy').
examples: The gentle music was accoying his troubled mind.
Soft words, accoying their fears, drifted through the room.

senses.definition: An archaic or variant past form of 'accoy', meaning soothed, calmed, or subdued.
examples: His anger was accoyld by her gentle apology.
The storm at last was accoyld, and silence fell upon the sea.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of the obsolete verb 'accoy', meaning soothes, calms, or subdues.
examples: Gentle music accoys the restless child.
Kind speech accoys even the fiercest temper.

senses.definition: The capital city of Ghana, on the Atlantic coast of West Africa.
examples: She moved from London to Accra for a new job.
Accra is known for its busy markets and coastal views.

senses.definition: To grow or increase by gradual addition; to accrete (rare and archaic).
examples: Over the years, dust would accrease on the old books.
His wealth did not come suddenly but seemed to accrease with each careful decision.

senses.definition: To officially recognize that a person or organization has met a particular standard.
examples: The university is accredited by a national education body.
Only accredited labs can perform these tests.

senses.definition: To send someone, especially a diplomat, to represent a government or organization.
examples: She was accredited as ambassador to Brazil.
Several journalists were accredited to the conference.

senses.definition: Deserving credit or praise; worthy of approval.
examples: Her performance was highly accreditable.
This success is accreditable to the whole team.

senses.definition: Able to be assigned or attributed to someone or something.
examples: The error is accreditable to poor planning.
These results are accreditable to several factors.

senses.definition: To accredit; to give official approval or authorization (rare).
examples: The board may accreditate new training centers once a year.
Several institutions were accredited or accreditated under the new rules.

senses.definition: Official approval that an organization, program, or person meets a particular standard.
examples: The college is waiting for accreditation from the national body.
Hospital accreditation is reviewed every three years.

senses.definition: Plural of accreditation; several official approvals or certifications.
examples: The university holds multiple international accreditations.
They listed all their professional accreditations on the website.

senses.definition: Officially recognized as meeting a particular standard.
examples: Only accredited courses are eligible for funding.
He studied at an accredited medical school.

senses.definition: Formally accepted or sent in an official position, especially as a diplomat or reporter.
examples: She is an accredited correspondent in the region.
Several accredited ambassadors attended the ceremony.

senses.definition: A person or organization that has been given accreditation (rare).
examples: Each accreditee must report annually to the board.
The accreditees are listed in the official register.

senses.definition: Present participle of accredit; officially recognizing that someone or something meets a standard.
examples: The agency is responsible for accrediting new schools.
Several bodies are accredited for accrediting medical programs.

senses.definition: The act of accrediting or the state of being accredited (rare, mostly historical).
examples: The accreditment of the college took several years.
Formal accreditment was necessary before they could open.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of accredit; officially recognizes that someone or something meets a standard.
examples: The council accredits all teacher‑training programs in the region.
The board accredits only institutions that meet its guidelines.

senses.definition: Growing or increasing by gradual external addition; relating to accretion (rare, technical).
examples: The rock showed accrementitial layers formed over centuries.
Accrementitial growth can be observed in certain minerals.

senses.definition: Growth or increase by gradual external addition; accretion (rare, technical).
examples: The slow accrementition of mineral deposits changed the cave walls.
Accrementition occurs as new material gathers over time.

senses.definition: To grow or increase by gradual external addition; to accrete (rare, literary or legal).
examples: The estate will accresce to the surviving partner.
Interest may accresce over time if the debt is unpaid.

senses.definition: Growth or increase by external addition; accretion (rare).
examples: The slow accrescence of responsibilities tired him.
Accrescence of coral created new islands over ages.

senses.definition: Plural of accrescence; multiple instances of growth or increase by addition.
examples: Various accrescences on the plant were carefully noted.
These social accrescences developed over many years.

senses.definition: Plural of accrescendo; musical passages that gradually grow louder (rare form).
examples: The piece is full of brief accrescendi and sudden pauses.
These accrescendi give the symphony its dramatic power.

senses.definition: A gradual increase in loudness in a piece of music, or the point of highest loudness.
examples: The orchestra rose to a dramatic accrescendo.
There is a long accrescendo in the middle of the song.

senses.definition: A gradual increase in intensity, force, or feeling in a situation.
examples: The debate reached an accrescendo of anger.
Their excitement grew in an accrescendo before the final match.

senses.definition: Growing or increasing after flowering, especially describing certain plant parts (technical).
examples: The accrescent calyx protects the fruit as it develops.
Some species have accrescent sepals that enlarge around the fruit.

senses.definition: Relating to or produced by accretion or gradual build‑up (rare, technical).
examples: Accretal growth can be observed along the river bank.
The accretal process formed a new layer of sediment.

senses.definition: To grow or form by gradual addition of new layers or parts.
examples: Over time, minerals accrete on the cave walls.
Small pieces of rock accrete to form larger bodies in space.

senses.definition: To cause something to grow by adding new parts or layers.
examples: Sediments accrete the river delta each year.
Dust particles gradually accrete the forming planet.

senses.definition: Past form of accrete; grew or formed by gradual addition.
examples: Material accreted around the young star.
A thick layer of dust had accreted on the surface.

senses.definition: Formed or increased by gradual external addition.
examples: The accreted crust forms part of the continent.
Accreted deposits lined the old pipes.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of accrete; grows or forms by gradual addition.
examples: Dust accretes around the forming star.
New sediment accretes at the river mouth each year.

senses.definition: Present participle of accrete; growing or forming by gradual addition.
examples: An accreting disk of gas surrounds the star.
The planet is still accreting material from the cloud.

senses.definition: The process of growth or increase by a gradual build‑up of layers or material.
examples: Beach formation is often due to the accretion of sand.
Accretion around the star eventually forms planets.

senses.definition: Something that has grown or been added as a layer or extra part.
examples: A hard accretion had formed inside the pipe.
The report is full of later accretions to the original story.

senses.definition: A gradual increase in amount, number, or influence over time.
examples: There has been an accretion of power in the central government.
The slow accretion of rules made the system complex.

senses.definition: Formed by or relating to accretion or gradual build‑up.
examples: The island is part of an accretionary wedge along the trench.
Accretionary processes shape many coastal regions.

senses.definition: Plural of accretion; things that have grown or been added as layers or extra parts.
examples: Over the years, many accretions have been added to the original building.
Mineral accretions narrowed the pipe.

senses.definition: Tending to increase or grow by gradual addition or accumulation.
examples: The company pursued an accretive growth strategy.
These deals are expected to be accretive to earnings.

senses.definition: An archaic or variant spelling of 'accrue', meaning to increase or be added over time, especially money or benefits.
examples: Interest will accrew on any unpaid balance.
The benefits that accrew from this policy may take years to appear.

senses.definition: Archaic or variant past form of 'accrue'; increased or was added over time.
examples: A large debt had accrewed over the years.
The advantages accrewed slowly but steadily.

senses.definition: Archaic or variant present participle of 'accrue'; increasing or being added over time.
examples: The accrewing interest will be paid annually.
Accrewing benefits may not be obvious at first.

senses.definition: Archaic or variant third person singular present of 'accrue'; increases or is added over time.
examples: Interest accrews on the unpaid sum.
Liability accrews as long as the contract remains in force.

senses.definition: To accuse or charge with a crime or fault (obsolete).
examples: They sought to accrimimate him without clear evidence.
No one wished to accrimimate an innocent man.

senses.definition: To take more power, rights, or property than one has a right to; to encroach or usurp (archaic).
examples: The ruler began to accroach powers that were not his.
They feared the council would accroach on their ancient rights.

senses.definition: Past form of accroach; took more power, rights, or property than was allowed (archaic).
examples: He accroached authority that belonged to the assembly.
The lords had accroached lands from the common people.

senses.definition: Present participle of accroach; taking more than one has a right to (archaic).
examples: An accroaching prince alarmed the council.
They accused him of accroaching upon the people's liberties.

senses.definition: The act of taking more power, rights, or property than one is entitled to; usurpation (archaic).
examples: The law forbade any accroachment of royal authority.
They resisted the accroachment of their freedoms.

senses.definition: A type of resin obtained from certain Australian trees, used in varnishes and other products (technical).
examples: Accroides was once used as a component in high‑quality varnish.
The lab tested samples of accroides resin.

senses.definition: Able to be added or accumulated over time, especially in finance or law.
examples: Accruable expenses must be recorded at year‑end.
The policy sets out the accrualable, or accruable, benefits.

senses.definition: The process of something, especially money or benefits, increasing or being added over time.
examples: The accrual of interest will continue until the loan is paid.
The policy explains the accrual of vacation days.

senses.definition: A record in accounting of money that has been earned or spent but not yet paid or received.
examples: We need to make an accrual for unpaid invoices.
End‑of‑year accruals must be checked carefully.

senses.definition: Amounts in accounting that show money earned or spent but not yet paid or received.
examples: The company reviews its accruals every month.
Year‑end accruals include unpaid salaries and utilities.

senses.definition: To increase or be added over time, especially money, interest, or benefits.
examples: Interest will accrue on the loan from day one.
Benefits accrue to employees after six months of service.

senses.definition: To come to someone as a direct or natural result.
examples: The advantages accrue to the whole community.
Rewards accrue to those who work hard.

senses.definition: Past form of accrue; increased or was added over time.
examples: Significant interest had accrued on the debt.
Several weeks of leave had accrued by the end of the year.

senses.definition: Describing money or benefits that have built up over time but are not yet paid or used.
examples: The company must pay all accrued salaries.
They listed their accrued liabilities in the report.

senses.definition: The act or process of accruing; the increase of something over time (rare).
examples: The accruement of such debts can be dangerous.
They discussed the accruement of benefits under the scheme.

senses.definition: Plural of accruement; several amounts or benefits that have accrued over time (rare).
examples: The accruements under the contract must be listed separately.
All accruements and charges are shown in the statement.

senses.definition: A person or thing to which something accrues, or that causes something to accrue (rare, chiefly legal).
examples: The benefits and duties of the contract fall on the accruer.
Each accruer must be named in the agreement.
